section.title: The People Put Away Foreign Wives
section.sectionRationale: Ezra's prayer moves the people to costly repentance. Shecaniah names a way forward, the assembly binds itself by covenant, and, through an orderly investigation, the men who had married into idolatry put away those marriages, some offering a guilt offering. The book ends starkly on a list of names and the note that children were affected. It is one movement: corporate repentance carried through at painful cost, and a sober ending that leaves us longing for a deeper cleansing.
section.thesis: Confronted by their unfaithfulness, the people repent in earnest, binding themselves by covenant to a painful separation from idolatrous marriages, yet the book's abrupt, sobering close shows that even radical reform cannot finally cleanse the heart, pointing beyond Ezra to the Redeemer who can.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Shecaniah's word of hope and the covenant, verses 1 to 5; the assembly summoned and charged, verses 6 to 14; the investigation carried out, verses 15 to 17; and the list of those who had married foreign women, verses 18 to 44.

units.label: Shecaniah's word of hope and the covenant
units.note: in spite of this, there is hope for Israel

verses.text: While Ezra prayed and made this confession, weeping and falling facedown before the house of God, a very large assembly of Israelites, men, women, and children, gathered around him, and the people wept bitterly as well.

Then Shecaniah son of Jehiel, an Elamite, said to Ezra: “We have been unfaithful to our God by marrying foreign women from the people of the land, yet in spite of this, there is {{miqveh|hope}} for Israel.

So now let us make a {{berit|covenant}} before our God to send away all the foreign wives and their children, according to the counsel of my lord and of those who tremble at the command of our God. Let it be done according to the Law.
Get up, for this matter is your responsibility, and we will support you. Be strong and take action!”
So Ezra got up and made the leading priests, Levites, and all Israel take an oath to do what had been said. And they took the oath.

Then Ezra withdrew from before the house of God and walked to the chamber of Jehohanan son of Eliashib. And while he stayed there, he ate no food and drank no water, because he was mourning over the unfaithfulness of the exiles.
And a proclamation was issued throughout Judah and Jerusalem that all the exiles should gather at Jerusalem.
Whoever failed to appear within three days would forfeit all his property, according to the counsel of the leaders and elders, and would himself be expelled from the assembly of the exiles.

So within the three days, all the men of Judah and Benjamin assembled in Jerusalem, and on the twentieth day of the ninth month, all the people sat in the square at the house of God, trembling regarding this matter and because of the heavy rain.
Then Ezra the priest stood up and said to them, “You have been unfaithful by marrying foreign women, adding to the guilt of Israel.
Now, therefore, make a {{yadah|confession}} to the LORD, the God of your fathers, and do His will. Separate yourselves from the people of the land and from your foreign wives.”
And the whole assembly responded in a loud voice: “Truly we must do as you say!
But there are many people here, and it is the rainy season. We are not able to stay out in the open. Nor is this the work of one or two days, for we have transgressed greatly in this matter.

Let our leaders represent the whole assembly. Then let everyone in our towns who has married a foreign woman come at an appointed time, together with the elders and judges of each town, until the fierce anger of our God in this matter is turned away from us.”

units.label: The investigation carried out
units.note: they dealt with all the men

verses.text: (Only Jonathan son of Asahel and Jahzeiah son of Tikvah, supported by Meshullam and Shabbethai the Levite, opposed this plan.)

So the exiles did as proposed. Ezra the priest selected men who were family heads, each of them identified by name, to represent their families. On the first day of the tenth month they launched the investigation,
and by the first day of the first month they had dealt with all the men who had married foreign women.

units.label: The list of those who had married foreign women
units.note: a guilt offering for their guilt

verses.text: Among the descendants of the priests who had married foreign women were found these descendants of Jeshua son of Jozadak and his brothers: Maaseiah, Eliezer, Jarib, and Gedaliah.
They pledged to send their wives away, and for their guilt they presented a ram from the flock as a {{asham|guilt offering}}.
From the descendants of Immer: Hanani and Zebadiah.
From the descendants of Harim: Maaseiah, Elijah, Shemaiah, Jehiel, and Uzziah.
From the descendants of Pashhur: Elioenai, Maaseiah, Ishmael, Nethanel, Jozabad, and Elasah.
Among the Levites: Jozabad, Shimei, Kelaiah (that is, Kelita), Pethahiah, Judah, and Eliezer.
From the singers: Eliashib. From the gatekeepers: Shallum, Telem, and Uri.
And among the other Israelites, from the descendants of Parosh: Ramiah, Izziah, Malchijah, Mijamin, Eleazar, Malchijah, and Benaiah.
From the descendants of Elam: Mattaniah, Zechariah, Jehiel, Abdi, Jeremoth, and Elijah.
From the descendants of Zattu: Elioenai, Eliashib, Mattaniah, Jeremoth, Zabad, and Aziza.
From the descendants of Bebai: Jehohanan, Hananiah, Zabbai, and Athlai.
From the descendants of Bani: Meshullam, Malluch, Adaiah, Jashub, Sheal, and Jeremoth.
From the descendants of Pahath-moab: Adna, Chelal, Benaiah, Maaseiah, Mattaniah, Bezalel, Binnui, and Manasseh.
From the descendants of Harim: Eliezer, Isshijah, Malchijah, Shemaiah, Shimeon,
Benjamin, Malluch, and Shemariah.
From the descendants of Hashum: Mattenai, Mattattah, Zabad, Eliphelet, Jeremai, Manasseh, and Shimei.
From the descendants of Bani: Maadai, Amram, Uel,
Benaiah, Bedeiah, Cheluhi,
Vaniah, Meremoth, Eliashib,
Mattaniah, Mattenai, and Jaasu.
From the descendants of Binnui: Shimei,
Shelemiah, Nathan, Adaiah,
Machnadebai, Shashai, Sharai,
Azarel, Shelemiah, Shemariah,
Shallum, Amariah, and Joseph.
And from the descendants of Nebo: Jeiel, Mattithiah, Zabad, Zebina, Jaddai, Joel, and Benaiah.
All these men had married foreign women, and some of them had children by these wives.

miqveh.gloss: Hope, expectation. Shecaniah declares that even after such sin, there is miqveh for Israel: repentance opens a door where guilt seemed to close every way. The word insists that the last thing about God's people, even in their failure, is not despair but hope grounded in God's mercy.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a solemn binding agreement. The people bind themselves by berit to turn from their unfaithfulness, an act of corporate repentance that renews their commitment to the LORD. Real repentance here is not a mood but a definite, accountable pledge before God.

yadah.gloss: To confess, acknowledge, give thanks. Ezra commands the people to make yadah, confession, to the LORD, owning their sin and returning to His will. True repentance begins with honest confession, agreeing with God about the wrong and turning from it.

asham.gloss: A guilt or reparation offering, presented to make amends for sin. The guilty priests offer a ram as an asham, acknowledging their debt and seeking atonement. The offering points beyond itself to Christ, of whom Isaiah says the LORD made His life a guilt offering, Isaiah 53:10.

basic.context: Ezra's public prayer breaks the people's hearts, and a crowd gathers weeping. Shecaniah speaks first: we have sinned, but there is still hope; let us covenant to put things right. The people bind themselves by oath, gather in Jerusalem in the pouring rain, and confess. Because the cases are many, they set up an orderly investigation over several months. Guilty men, including priests, put away the idolatrous marriages, and some offer a guilt offering. The book ends abruptly with a long list of names and the sobering note that children were affected. The question is: what does real repentance cost, and can even this fully cleanse the heart?
basic.hermeneutics: This is a difficult narrative that describes what the community did; it does not simply command us to imitate every action. The marriages were to idolaters and endangered the covenant people's very survival, and the response was extreme and painful. Read in light of the whole Bible, this is not a warrant for divorce, which God hates, nor for racial separation. The New Testament gives a fuller path: a believing spouse sanctifies a home. The enduring lesson is the deadly seriousness of sin and the costliness of genuine repentance.
basic.originalLanguages: Shecaniah insists there is still miqveh, hope, for Israel. The people respond by making a berit, a covenant, to turn from their sin, and Ezra calls them to yadah, to confess, to the LORD. The guilty priests bring a ram as an asham, a guilt offering, seeking atonement.
basic.typology: The guilt offering points to Christ, made a guilt offering for us, who alone can truly cleanse. The book's unfinished, sobering ending points to the need for a deeper reform than the law can accomplish, fulfilled in the new covenant Christ brings.
basic.christ: This chapter shows repentance at its most costly, and yet its abrupt, painful ending, names and affected children, leaves us feeling that something is still unresolved. The law can expose sin and even cut it away, but it cannot change the heart, and indeed the same sin resurfaces later under Nehemiah. That ache points us to Christ. Where the ram was offered as a guilt offering, Jesus becomes our guilt offering, bearing sin fully. Where Ezra's reform was partial, Christ writes God's law on the heart and cleanses His people completely.
basic.god: God is holy and takes sin with deadly seriousness, yet He is the God of hope who receives the repentant. He turns His fierce anger away from a people who confess and return, and He provides, in the guilt offering, a foreshadowing of the atonement He will accomplish Himself.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 7:12-14 shows a believing spouse sanctifying the marriage, a fuller new-covenant path. Isaiah 53:10 makes the Servant a guilt offering. And Jeremiah 31:33 promises the law written on the heart.
basic.oneStory: The book of Ezra ends with real but incomplete repentance: the people turn from sin at great cost, yet the heart remains unchanged and the same sin will return. This leaves us longing for a better Redeemer, Christ, who is Himself the guilt offering and who cleanses and renews the heart under a new and better covenant.

context.literary: The final chapter shifts to the third person and moves from Ezra's prayer to communal action: Shecaniah's proposal, the covenant oath, the rainy assembly, the investigation, and the closing register of names. The book ends without a neat resolution, on a stark list and the note that children were involved, a deliberately unsettling conclusion that refuses to gloss over the cost of sin.
context.historical: In the tiny, threatened post-exilic community, marriages into surrounding idolatrous peoples posed a real danger of assimilation and apostasy, the very sin that had brought the exile. The measures reflect an emergency response led by priestly and civil authority, with the people's own consent by oath. The mention of a guilt offering shows the guilty seeking atonement, not merely legal compliance.
context.canonical: The reform proves partial: Nehemiah 13 records the same sin resurfacing a generation later, underscoring that external measures cannot finally renew the heart. The chapter thus deepens the Bible's longing for the new covenant, promised by Jeremiah and Ezekiel and fulfilled in Christ, in which God cleanses His people and writes His law within. The guilt offering here anticipates the once-for-all offering of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the community's costly, corporate repentance from covenant-endangering unfaithfulness, and, by ending on a sobering note, to expose both the seriousness of sin and the limits of reform that cannot change the heart.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific, extreme historical response to an emergency of idolatry; it is not a prescription for divorce or ethnic separation. Scripture elsewhere says God hates divorce and that a believing spouse sanctifies a marriage. What endures is the call to radical, honest repentance and separation from whatever draws the heart from God.

debates.question: Was the mass sending away of foreign wives and children right?

views.label: A necessary emergency measure
views.case: The marriages were to idolaters and threatened the survival and holiness of the covenant people through whom the Messiah would come; led by priestly authority and undertaken by the people's own covenant, it was a drastic but justified defense of the community's faithfulness in a unique crisis.

views.label: A genuinely hard, imperfect human response
views.case: The narrative reports what was done without praising every element, and its bleak ending, affected children, invites lament; some note the tension with God's hatred of divorce, reading the episode as a painful, less-than-ideal reaction to a desperate situation rather than a timeless command.

debates.bearing: However one judges the specifics, the passage displays the deadly seriousness of sin and the costliness of repentance, and drives us to Christ, who cleanses fully and offers a better way than either compromise or tragedy.

debates.question: Why does the book of Ezra end so abruptly on a list of names?

views.label: A sobering, deliberate close
views.case: The unresolved ending, with its roll of the guilty and the note about children, leaves the reader feeling the lingering cost of sin and the incompleteness of the reform, refusing a tidy, triumphant conclusion.

views.label: A faithful historical record
views.case: The naming preserves an honest account of who was involved and how seriously the matter was handled, in keeping with the book's concern for careful records and real accountability before God.

debates.bearing: Either way, the ending points beyond itself: the ministry of the law can expose and excise sin but cannot renew the heart, leaving us longing for the Redeemer who can, and who comes in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words trace the shape of repentance. Against the despair of exposed sin, Shecaniah insists there is still miqveh, hope, for Israel, for repentance opens a door where guilt seemed to seal every exit. The people respond not with a passing feeling but with a berit, a covenant, a solemn, accountable pledge to turn from their unfaithfulness, and Ezra summons them to yadah, to confess, agreeing with God about their sin and returning to His will. When the guilty priests act, they bring a ram as an asham, a guilt offering, seeking atonement rather than mere legal compliance, and that word reaches forward to Isaiah's Servant, whose life the LORD would make a guilt offering for the sins of many. Hope, covenant, confession, guilt offering: the vocabulary of real repentance, yet an atonement by rams that could never finally cleanse, leaving the story open toward Christ.

typology.type: The ram offered as a guilt offering
typology.fulfillment: Christ, whom the LORD made a guilt offering for sin, bearing it fully and once for all, Isaiah 53:10 and Hebrews 10:12-14.

typology.type: The partial reform that could not change the heart
typology.fulfillment: The new covenant in which God writes His law on the heart and cleanses His people, Jeremiah 31:33 and Ezekiel 36:26-27.

typology.type: Hope declared to a guilty people
typology.fulfillment: The gospel hope offered to sinners in Christ, in whom there is now no condemnation, Romans 8:1 and 1 Timothy 1:15-16.

study.christ: The book closes on repentance that is real and costly, and yet strangely unresolved: a list of names, a guilt offering by rams, and children caught in the wreckage of sin. The very incompleteness preaches the gospel by contrast. External reform, even sincere and drastic, cannot renew a heart, which is why the same sin reappears under Nehemiah. Jesus is the answer the ending cries out for. He becomes the true guilt offering, bearing sin fully so that no ram ever could again. He brings the new covenant, writing God's law within and giving a new heart, so that repentance is not merely enforced from outside but flows from a renewed nature. And He is the hope Shecaniah glimpsed: for guilty people there is hope, not in our reforms, but in the Redeemer who cleanses completely.
study.god: God is holy, and He will not treat covenant unfaithfulness as trivial; the chapter refuses to soften the gravity of sin or its cost. Yet He is also the God of hope, who does not despise a repentant people but receives their confession and turns His fierce anger away. In providing the guilt offering, He points to the atonement He will ultimately make Himself. The God of Ezra is both the righteous Judge who takes sin seriously and the merciful Savior who opens a door of hope, a God whose full character is revealed at the cross.

crossReferences.note: The LORD makes the Servant's life a guilt offering, the reality foreshadowed by the ram offered here.
Under the new covenant a believing spouse sanctifies the marriage and children, a fuller path than this emergency measure.
The same sin of intermarriage resurfaces later, showing that external reform could not finally change the heart.
God's promise of a new heart and His Spirit, the deeper cleansing this partial reform could not achieve.

study.oneStory: Ezra ends with genuine but incomplete repentance: God's people turn from sin at real and painful cost, yet the heart is unchanged and the same sin will return. The abrupt, sobering close leaves us reaching for something more, a Redeemer who is Himself the guilt offering and who cleanses and renews from within. That is Christ, who fulfills what the law could only expose, bringing the new covenant in which God's people are truly made holy and given lasting hope.

quiz.q: What did Shecaniah declare to Ezra amid the people's weeping?

options.text: That although they had sinned, there was still hope for Israel if they repented.
options.note: Verse 2 voices hope for Israel and proposes a covenant to put things right.

options.text: That the situation was hopeless and beyond remedy.
options.note: The opposite: he insists there is hope in spite of the sin.

options.text: That Ezra was to blame for the whole crisis.
options.note: He calls Ezra to lead the remedy, not blames him for the sin.

options.text: That the sin did not really matter.
options.note: He openly names it as unfaithfulness that must be addressed.

quiz.q: How did the people formalize their repentance?

options.text: They bound themselves by a covenant and oath to turn from their unfaithfulness.
options.note: Verses 3-5 record the covenant and oath before God.

options.text: They simply felt sorry and moved on.
options.note: Their repentance was a definite, accountable pledge, not a passing feeling.

options.text: They paid a fine to the Persian king.
options.note: The response was a covenant before God, not a payment to the king.

options.text: They blamed their leaders and did nothing.
options.note: The leaders led, and the people acted by oath.

quiz.q: How was the matter handled given the many cases and the rain?

options.text: Through an orderly investigation over several months, with leaders representing the assembly.
options.note: Verses 13-17 describe the appointed, orderly process.

options.text: By a single hasty decision in one afternoon.
options.note: The people themselves said it could not be done in a day; it took months.

options.text: By ignoring the problem until it went away.
options.note: They addressed it deliberately, not by ignoring it.

options.text: By a violent riot in the square.
options.note: The process was orderly and accountable, not violent.

quiz.q: What did some of the guilty priests offer, and what does it point to?

options.text: A ram as a guilt offering, pointing to Christ, made a guilt offering for us.
options.note: Verse 19 and Isaiah 53:10 connect the guilt offering to Christ.

options.text: A gold statue, pointing to their wealth.
options.note: It was a guilt offering seeking atonement, not a display of wealth.

options.text: Nothing at all; they refused to repent.
options.note: They pledged to act and presented a guilt offering.

options.text: A Passover lamb, unrelated to guilt.
options.note: It was specifically a guilt offering, an asham, for their sin.

quiz.q: What does the abrupt, sobering ending of Ezra teach us?

options.text: That even costly reform cannot change the heart, leaving us longing for the cleansing Christ brings.
options.note: The unresolved ending points beyond the law to the new covenant in Christ.

options.text: That the people's repentance solved the problem permanently.
options.note: The same sin resurfaces under Nehemiah, showing the reform was partial.

options.text: That God had given up on His people.
options.note: God receives their repentance; the point is the need for deeper cleansing, not abandonment.

options.text: That external rules alone can perfect God's people.
options.note: The ending shows the opposite: rules expose sin but cannot renew the heart.

questions: Shecaniah insisted there was still hope even after real sin. Where do you need to hear that repentance opens a door of hope rather than only condemnation?
The people made a definite covenant rather than merely feeling sorry. What would it look like for your repentance to become concrete and accountable instead of vague regret?
Their repentance was genuinely costly and painful. Is there a sin you are unwilling to deal with because turning from it would cost you something dear?
The reform could not finally change the heart, and the same sin returned later. How does this drive you to depend on Christ and the new heart He gives rather than on willpower alone?
The guilt offering pointed forward to Christ, our guilt offering. How does resting in His finished, complete atonement free you to repent honestly and without despair?

translationNotes.note: Let it be done according to the Law appeals to the community's understanding of God's commands about idolatrous marriage; the measure is framed as covenant faithfulness, not personal cruelty.
A guilt offering renders the Hebrew asham, a reparation offering seeking atonement; Isaiah 53:10 uses the same word of the suffering Servant, pointing to Christ.
The final clause is difficult in Hebrew; the BSB footnote notes an alternate reading, and they sent them away with their children. Either way the book ends soberly on the lingering cost of sin.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ezra 10:1-44; all 44 verses included. Em dashes in verse 1 were rendered as commas per house style. Chapter 10 is Hebrew throughout. No em or en dashes remain.
